title: Recycling plastic bags and film
subtitle: What counts as film plastic, where the drop-off points are, and what cannot be recycled
excerpt: Plastic film and bags are not accepted in kerbside recycling in most UK councils because they wrap around sorting machinery. But supermarket carrier bag drop points accept a wide range of film plastics beyond just carrier bags. This covers what is accepted, where the drop-off points are, and the items that look like film but are not recyclable.
sourceNotes: WRAP UK plastic film recycling guidance. Wrap Recycle Now film plastics accepted list. Tesco, Sainsbury's, and Waitrose carrier bag drop point accepted items lists.

glossaryTerms.definition: Flexible plastic films and bags (polyethylene PE and polypropylene PP), as distinct from rigid plastics. Examples: carrier bags, bread bags, bubble wrap, cling film, pasta packets. Most are LDPE (low-density polyethylene) or HDPE.

content.text: Soft plastics (what the recycling industry calls flexible plastics) account for approximately 1.5 kg per household per week of non-kerbside-recyclable plastic waste in the UK. Most of it is polyethylene (PE), which is recyclable when collected separately and kept dry. The barrier is not the material; it is the collection route. Most major supermarkets have accepted carrier bag drops since 2019 and most now accept a wide range of

Accepted at most major supermarket drop points: carrier bags, bags for life, bread bags, pasta bags, rice bags, magazine wrapping, cereal bag liners, fruit and veg bags, bubble wrap, newspaper bags, toilet roll outer wrap, and frozen food bags. All must be empty and dry.

Check the scheme label at the drop point: some stores also accept resealable food bags (Ziplock type), salad bags, and black sacks. The list varies by retailer and is updated. The RecycleNow.com postcode finder shows the nearest soft plastics drop point and the current accepted items list for that specific location.
What looks like film but is not recyclable at these points

Crisp packets: these are metallised films (a polyester base with an aluminium coating), not polyethylene. Not accepted at supermarket drop points. Collect for specialist recycling via TerraCycle or Walkers Recycle on the Go.
Cling film: technically recyclable PE, but most drop points do not accept it because it is typically contaminated with food residue. Check locally.

Biodegradable or compostable bags (e.g. Vegware, Bio-Bag): these look like plastic film but are made from PLA or PBAT and should not go into the plastic film recycling stream. They contaminate the PE recycling process. Compost in an industrial composting facility or general waste only.
Black bin liner bags: accepted at most drop points if the film is clean polyethylene. Used kitchen bin liners with food residue are not accepted.

Keep a dedicated bag hanging inside a cupboard door for soft plastic collection. Stuff clean, dry film plastics into it during the week. Take to the supermarket drop point on the next routine shop. The constraint is keeping the items dry and food-free. Contaminated items are rejected at the recycling plant and the whole bag may be turned away.
